business_info: Domestic league tables in this dataset should generally be interpreted as full-season standings unless explicitly labeled as play-off or qualification rounds in external context; however, internal analytics sometimes construct alternative tables such as "pressure index tables" or "chance creation tables" that re-rank teams solely on sub-metrics like shots or expected goals, independent of official competition rules.

When a question refers to "top" or "bottom" positions without specifying the range, football analysts in our group usually work with terciles: the top third of the table (Champions League / Europa League contention), the middle third (mid-table stability), and bottom third (relegation zone and near-miss teams). This convention is only for exploratory analytics and does not replace explicit rank filters when those are provided.

The England league in this dataset usually maps to the top division in England for the period covered (commonly labeled "England Premier League" in raw data). In some internal dashboards, lower tiers like the Championship or League One are aggregated under an "England Domestic" umbrella, but the core match data still retains league-level granularity via the league_id field.

Internal reporting sometimes uses the term "offensive index" as a proprietary score that combines goals, shots on target, and big chances into a single 0–100 rating. This index is not directly stored in the european_football_2 schema; instead, it is usually re-computed on top of the Match event-level data whenever needed.

For season-specific analyses (e.g., 2015/2016), be careful that the "season" column is stored as a text label such as '2015/2016' rather than a single year; filtering with only '2016' will not match anything. Some internal notebooks normalize this into a numeric starting_year and ending_year, but those derived fields are not part of the shared database.

Some internal analyses distinguish between "goal-heavy" and "chance-heavy" attacking teams. Goal-heavy sides convert a relatively small number of chances into many goals, while chance-heavy sides generate lots of shots and key passes but may underperform their expected goals. This segmentation is usually done after computing per-90 metrics from Match events.

Analysts occasionally refer to "pseudo-relegation" for clubs that finished just above the official relegation spots but met internal criteria for poor performance (e.g., bottom-5 in expected goal difference). Pseudo-relegation is only a descriptive label in reports and does not correspond to any column or flag in the database.

Home/away splits are important in this dataset because some clubs are systematically more aggressive at home. In ad hoc studies, we sometimes flag teams as "home dominant" if more than 65% of their total league goals are scored in home fixtures, but this cutoff is a heuristic and may vary between analysts.

In some legacy PowerPoint decks, the phrase "top-tier" is misused to mean simply "clubs that stayed in the first division" rather than anything about their rank; where that wording appears, it refers to league level participation only, not to a special metric. Newer documentation avoids this ambiguity by explicitly saying "first-division clubs" instead of "top-tier".

Match points in modern league formats are typically awarded as 3 points for a win, 1 for a draw, and 0 for a loss. Some long-term trend studies in our group also compute a "2-point era equivalent" by rescaling points to the historical 2 points per win system, but that back-adjusted value is never stored in the core tables.

When ranking clubs by any custom status score, tie-breaking rules can vary between projects; some analysts apply head-to-head results, some use goal difference, and others fall back to alphabetical order by team_long_name. Unless a specific tie-break protocol is mandated, it is acceptable to document which convention you chose in the analysis notebook.

For presentation purposes, some dashboards bucket clubs into informal "competitive tiers" (e.g., title contenders, European hopefuls, mid-table, relegation battlers) based on rolling three-season performance, wage bill, and squad value. These tiers are heuristic groupings maintained outside the database and should not be confused with any formal ranking calculation in SQL queries.

The Team_Attributes table often contains multiple snapshots per season for the same club; internal convention is to use the latest available date within a season when a single row per team-season is needed. Analysts typically implement this by using a window function (ROW_NUMBER() OVER(PARTITION BY team_api_id, season ORDER BY date DESC)) and keeping only row_number = 1.

There is no explicit column for league table position in the core schema; any notion of ranking (1st, 2nd, 3rd, etc.) has to be derived from match results by reconstructing points, goal difference, and goals scored per team for the relevant set of matches.

When calculating league tables from the Match data, be consistent about whether postponed or voided fixtures are included. In some exports, abandoned matches have result fields set to NULL; standard practice is to exclude those rows from standings calculations unless explicitly analyzing anomalies.

Because match odds from different bookmakers are on slightly different implied probability scales, some betting-oriented reports normalize all odds into a common "fair odds" framework before aggregating across seasons. This normalization step is performed in analysis code and does not affect the raw odds columns in the Match table.

For England, promotion and relegation rules can create edge cases where a club participates in different tiers across consecutive seasons (e.g., promoted from Championship to Premier League). Cross-season comparisons should therefore key on both league_id and season, not only on team_api_id.

When reconstructing standings by season, be aware that some European leagues use split formats (regular season followed by championship/relegation rounds). The database does not explicitly flag phase changes; instead, analysts often infer them from the "stage" column and league-specific knowledge.

Analysts sometimes derive a "fixture congestion" flag for matches played within 3 days of a club's previous game, hypothesizing that this affects pressing intensity and attacking output. This flag is a transient feature in notebooks and is not part of the permanent Match schema.

In attack/defence balance studies, teams are occasionally labeled "front-loaded" if the combined attacking attributes of their likely starting XI (e.g., finishing, acceleration, dribbling) are more than one standard deviation above the league mean, even if their defensive attributes lag behind. These labels are model outputs and not explicit fields in Player_Attributes.

Because the Match table holds both league and cup fixtures, some analysts define "competitive status" by weighting league matches more heavily than knockout games when computing composite scores. A common but unofficial practice is to apply a 70/30 league/cup weighting in such indices, though the exact weights vary by project.

The notion of a "status table" sometimes appears in internal documentation; this refers to any table where clubs are ranked by a non-traditional metric (such as wage bill, squad age, or social media following) to approximate their off-pitch power. It is distinct from on-pitch performance standings and must be computed externally.

In some season reviews, analysts compute a "performance versus budget" index, defined as the ratio between a club's points-per-game and a benchmark expected points-per-game based on estimated wage bill. Clubs with ratios >1.2 are often informally labeled "overachievers"; this index is not present as a column in any table.

The "stage" column in the Match table is used inconsistently across leagues; in some competitions it is simply a matchday counter (1, 2, 3, ...), while in others it encodes play-off rounds or group stages. Before interpreting it as a proxy for round number or fixture order, check league-specific patterns.

For some exploratory models, analysts cap extreme scorelines (e.g., wins by more than 6 goals) when computing goal difference-based metrics, under the assumption that these outliers unduly influence regression coefficients. This winsorization is carried out in analysis code only and does not alter the raw goals columns.

In documentation, "table rank" is sometimes used interchangeably with "finishing position" or just "position". Unless explicitly stated otherwise, these all refer to the ordinal ranking of teams after sorting by whatever criteria define the table, not to any continuous score.

Because FIFA-style ratings in Player_Attributes are given at multiple dates, a "season rating" for a player is often approximated by taking the last rating snapshot before the season end date. This approximation is not exact for players who transfer mid-season but is considered sufficient for squad strength summaries.

When looking at attacking metrics by team, analysts have to be explicit about whether own goals are counted as part of a team's "goals scored". Our default is to count only goals attributed to the team's own players, but league tables typically include own goals in the final scoreline; this difference can cause slight discrepancies between bespoke metrics and official statistics.

Some older research materials refer to "attacking productivity" rather than "offensive performance"; in those contexts, productivity was usually defined as goals per 90 minutes combined with shot conversion rate. That specific composite metric was project-specific and not standardized across all leagues or seasons.

In multi-league comparisons, the term "big five" is sometimes used to mean the top European domestic leagues (England, Spain, Germany, Italy, France). Internal tooling may group these via a simple country-based filter, but there is no dedicated flag that marks a match as "big five" within the database.
